sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **break down** — stop working (for a machine, etc)
**make up** — invent an explanation or excuse
**throw away** — put something in a rubbish bin

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: The doors are ......, so they open when someone walks towards them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is doors that open by themselves, so the correct answer is "automatic".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: I took my ...... to the library so I could finish my essay.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is portable computer for essays, so the correct answer is "laptop".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The team carried out years of ...... before testing the new medicine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is years of study before testing, so the correct answer is "research".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Do you know how to ...... this new 3D printer?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is use/work a machine, so the correct answer is "operate".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The physics teacher showed us some special ...... for the experiment.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is tools for an experiment, so the correct answer is "equipment".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Most students prefer ...... books because they can read them on a tablet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is read on a tablet, so the correct answer is "digital".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The instructions were so ...... that nobody understood them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is nobody understood, so the correct answer is "complicated".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: Astronomers hope to ...... new planets outside our solar system.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is find something new, so the correct answer is "discover".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: This little kitchen ...... can cut vegetables in seconds.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is small useful device, so the correct answer is "gadget".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "gadget" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "This little kitchen ...... can cut vegetables in seconds.".

items.sentence: The robot has a ...... design that makes it different from all the others.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is different from all others, so the correct answer is "unique".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The scientists worked late in the ...... to finish the test.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Scientists work in a laboratory.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: New ...... can help doctors find illnesses earlier.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Modern medicine uses new technology.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: how long the experiment will take?
items.originalSentence: Can you **estimate / involve** how long the experiment will take?
items.completedSentence: Can you estimate how long the experiment will take?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "estimate" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "estimate" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "involve" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.involve: "involve" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("how long the experiment will take?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: flash, and the screen went black.
items.originalSentence: There was a **sudden / minimum** flash, and the screen went black.
items.completedSentence: There was a sudden flash, and the screen went black.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "sudden" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "sudden" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "minimum" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.minimum: "minimum" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("flash, and the screen went black.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: The toy is made of strong
items.originalSentence: The toy is made of strong **hardware / plastic**.
items.completedSentence: The toy is made of strong plastic .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "plastic" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "plastic" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "hardware" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.hardware: "hardware" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: We need the **exact / artificial** weight of the object.
items.completedSentence: We need the exact weight of the object.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "exact" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "exact" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "artificial" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.artificial: "artificial" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("weight of the object.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The computer **screen / effect** is too bright.
items.completedSentence: The computer screen is too bright.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "screen" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "screen" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "effect" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.effect: "effect" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("is too bright.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Using less electricity will **decrease / invent** our monthly bills.
items.completedSentence: Using less electricity will decrease our monthly bills.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "decrease" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "decrease" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "invent" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.invent: "invent" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("our monthly bills.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: This program can **run / operate** on any modern laptop.
items.completedSentence: This program can run on any modern laptop.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "run" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "run" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "operate" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.operate: "operate" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("on any modern laptop.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: The machine doesn't work well because it
items.originalSentence: The machine doesn't work well because it **lacks / estimates** power.
items.completedSentence: The machine doesn't work well because it lacks power.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "lacks" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "lacks" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "estimates" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.estimates: "estimates" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("power.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: Please ...... the projector after the presentation.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is switch off, so the correct answer is "turn off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "turn off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I ...... an interesting article about robots while browsing online.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is find by chance, so the correct answer is "came across".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "came across", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Let's ...... what time the science exhibition opens.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is discover information, so the correct answer is "find out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "find out", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: If the lift ......, we will have to use the stairs.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is stop working, so the correct answer is "breaks down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "breaks down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Don't ...... old phones; they can be recycled.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is dispose of, so the correct answer is "throw away".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "throw away", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The student tried to ...... a story about why his homework was late.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is invent a false story, so the correct answer is "make up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "make up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Could you ...... the lights? It's getting dark.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is switch on, so the correct answer is "turn on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "turn on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Don't ...... that handle or the machine may break.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is remove by pulling, so the correct answer is "pull off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "pull off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with one phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with one phrase.

items.sentence: After three failed attempts, the robot worked ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is finally, after waiting, so the correct answer is "at last".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "at last", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We found the missing cable ...... behind the desk.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is unexpectedly, so the correct answer is "by chance".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "by chance", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "by chance" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: ......, solar power will become more common.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is giving an opinion, so the correct answer is "in my opinion".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in my opinion", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The vending machine is ......, so don't put any money in it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not working, so the correct answer is "out of order".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "out of order", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We tried several designs and, ......, chose the simplest one.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is after trying several, so the correct answer is "in the end".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Robots may do many dangerous jobs ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is later time, so the correct answer is "in the future".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in the future", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: Word patterns
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: This phone is different ...... the one I had before.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Different from the one before.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence: The box was full ...... old computer parts.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Full of old computer parts.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "of" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "The box was full ...... old computer parts.".

items.sentence: We began the lesson ...... a short video about inventions.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Start with a short video.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Connect the keyboard ...... the computer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Connect to the computer.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: You must disconnect the machine ...... the power supply first.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Disconnect from the power supply.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Too much screen time can result ...... headaches.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Result in headaches.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: There is a big difference ...... hardware and software.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Difference between hardware and software.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "between" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "There is a big difference ...... hardware and software.".

items.sentence: Have you got an idea ...... how to fix this gadget?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Idea about how to fix it.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: A number ...... scientists disagree with the theory.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A number of scientists.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "of" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "A number ...... scientists disagree with the theory.".

items.sentence: What is the reason ...... the decrease in temperature?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Reason for the decrease.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The soup was ...... when I took it off the cooker. (BOIL)
Our old ...... stopped working, so we had no hot water. (BOIL)
Marie Curie was a famous ...... . (SCIENCE)
We did a ...... experiment in class today. (CHEMIST)
The report ended with a clear ...... . (CONCLUDE)
The museum had a ...... exhibition about early computers. (FASCINATE)
The first moon landing was a ...... moment. (HISTORY)
The scientist checked the ...... three times. (MEASURE)

exercises.title: Multiple choice
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with a word from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a word from the box.

items.sentence: This old computer can't ...... the newest games.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Software runs games on a computer.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The experiment failed because of a ...... of clean water.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "maximum" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "The ...... speed of this electric scooter is 25 km/h.".

items.sentence: The project will ...... designing and testing a new app.

items.sentence: The new medicine had a positive ...... on the patients.

exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with one suitable word.

items.sentence: In the ......, the team decided to repeat the experiment.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Please don't throw ...... batteries with normal rubbish.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is dispose of, so the correct answer is "away".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The ticket machine is out of ...... again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.

items.sentence: People may travel to Mars in the ...... .

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
